questions.question:
In a bottom-up evaluation of a syntax directed definition, inherited attributes can
A. always be evaluated
B. be evaluated only if the definition is L-attributed
C. be evaluated only if the definition has synthesized attributes
D. never be evaluated
questions.answer: B. be evaluated only if the definition is L-attributed
questions.to_find: Under what condition can inherited attributes be evaluated in a bottom-up evaluation of an SDD

understand.plain: Syntax Directed Definitions (SDDs) associate attributes with grammar symbols. Synthesized attributes flow up the parse tree (from children to parent) while inherited attributes flow down (from parent to children). Bottom-up parsers naturally support synthesized attributes. The question asks when inherited attributes can also be handled bottom-up.

keywords.explain: An attribute whose value at a node is computed from the node's parent and/or siblings in the parse tree.
keywords.example: In 'int x, y', the type 'int' is inherited by x and y from the declaration head.

keywords.explain: An SDD is L-attributed if each inherited attribute of a symbol on the right-hand side of a production depends only on attributes of symbols to its LEFT in the production and on inherited attributes of the head (parent).
keywords.example: A -> B C where C.inh depends only on B.syn or A.inh — this is L-attributed.

keywords.explain: Attributes are evaluated as the parser reduces. The parser stack holds semantic values computed so far.
keywords.example: In shift-reduce parsing, when a production A -> B C is reduced, B and C values are on the stack.

understand.visual_alt: Parse tree showing inherited attributes flowing top-down while synthesized attributes flow bottom-up.

given.aim: Determine when inherited attributes can be evaluated during bottom-up parsing.

terms.meaning: Computed from children — naturally supported bottom-up.
terms.connects: Always evaluable bottom-up; the baseline case.

terms.meaning: Computed from parent or left siblings — normally require top-down traversal.
terms.example: T.type inherited from D.type in declaration grammar
terms.connects: Problematic in bottom-up parsing because parent is not yet reduced.

terms.meaning: Inherited attributes depend only on left siblings and parent's inherited attributes; can be simulated on a stack.
terms.example: In A -> X_1 X_2 ... X_n, X_i.inh depends on X_1, ..., X_{i-1} synthesized attributes and A.inh.
terms.connects: This left-to-right dependency matches the order items appear on the parser stack, enabling bottom-up simulation.

given.plan: Recall the theory of attribute evaluation orders. S-attributed SDDs (only synthesized) trivially work bottom-up. L-attributed SDDs are a superset that additionally allows inherited attributes computed left-to-right, which can be simulated during bottom-up parsing using the stack. Other inherited attributes (non-L-attributed) cannot be evaluated bottom-up.

steps.title: Recall S-attributed and L-attributed classes
steps.apply: An S-attributed SDD uses only synthesized attributes and works naturally with bottom-up parsers. An L-attributed SDD allows inherited attributes that depend only on left siblings and the inherited attributes of the parent production head. This left-to-right flow can be simulated on the parser's value stack.
steps.note: L-attributed is a strict superset of S-attributed; every S-attributed SDD is also L-attributed.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.apply:
A. 'Always' — false; arbitrary inherited attributes cannot be computed bottom-up because the parent node hasn't been created yet when we need the value.
B. 'Only if L-attributed' — correct; L-attributed allows inherited attributes arranged left-to-right, which exactly corresponds to the stack state during bottom-up parsing.
C. 'Only if it has synthesized attributes' — irrelevant; having synthesized attributes doesn't enable arbitrary inherited attribute evaluation.
D. 'Never' — false; L-attributed definitions DO permit inherited attributes bottom-up.
steps.note: The standard compiler theory result: L-attributed SDDs can be evaluated in one left-to-right pass, making them compatible with both top-down (LL) and bottom-up (LR) parsers.

solution.result: Inherited attributes can be evaluated in a bottom-up evaluation only if the definition is L-attributed. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: L-attributed SDD: inherited attribute of X_i in A -> X_1...X_n depends only on A's inherited attributes and synthesized attributes of X_1,...,X_{i-1}. This left-to-right dependency can be simulated on the LR parser stack.

questions.question:
Consider the syntax directed definition shown below. Here, gen is a function that generates the output code, and newtemp is a function that returns the name of a new temporary variable on every call. Assume that t_1, t_2, t_3 are the temporary variable names generated by newtemp.

Production | Semantic action
S -> id = E   | { gen(id, ':=', E.place); }
E -> E_1 + E_2 | { t = newtemp(); gen(t, ':=', E_1.place, '+', E_2.place); E.place = t; }
E -> id       | { E.place = id; }

For the statement a = b + c + d, the 3-address code sequence generated by this definition is:
A. t_1 := b + c
   t_2 := t_1 + d
   a := t_2
B. t_1 := c + d
   t_2 := b + t_1
   a := t_2
C. t_1 := b + c
   t_2 := t_1 + d
   t_3 := t_2
   a := t_3
D. t_1 := b + c
   a := t_1 + d
questions.to_find: The 3-address code generated for a = b + c + d using the given SDD

understand.plain: The syntax-directed definition specifies how to generate 3-address code for assignments and additions. The grammar is left-recursive for E (E -> E_1 + E_2), so b + c + d is parsed as (b + c) + d. Each addition allocates a new temporary via newtemp and emits a 3-address instruction via gen. The final assignment uses gen for id := E.place.

keywords.explain: An intermediate representation where each instruction has at most three operands: result := op1 op op2.
keywords.example: t1 := a + b is a valid 3-address instruction.

keywords.explain: Returns a fresh temporary variable name each call (t_1, t_2, t_3, ... in sequence).
keywords.example: First call returns t_1, second returns t_2.

keywords.explain: Synthesized attribute holding the name of the variable/temporary that stores the value of expression E.
keywords.example: After E -> id, E.place = id (the variable name itself).

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="340" height="260"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width="340" height="260" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="170" y="28" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">Parse Tree: a = (b+c)+d</text><text x="170" y="60" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">S</text><line x1="170" y1="65" x2="170" y2="85" stroke="#2C2C2A" stroke-width="2"/><text x="170" y="100" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">E (t_2)</text><line x1="170" y1="105" x2="100" y2="125" stroke="#2C2C2A" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="170" y1="105" x2="240" y2="125" stroke="#2C2C2A" stroke-width="2"/><text x="100" y="140" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">E (t_1)</text><text x="240" y="140" text-anchor="middle" class="ink lbl">d</text><line x1="100" y1="145" x2="60" y2="165" stroke="#2C2C2A" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="100" y1="145" x2="140" y2="165" stroke="#2C2C2A" stroke-width="2"/><text x="60" y="180" text-anchor="middle" class="ink sub">b</text><text x="140" y="180" text-anchor="middle" class="ink sub">c</text><text x="170" y="230" text-anchor="middle" class="mut sub">t_1:=b+c, t_2:=t_1+d, a:=t_2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Parse tree for a = b + c + d showing left-associative grouping: S -> id = E, E -> (E_1:b+c) + E_2:d. Temporary t_1 holds b+c, t_2 holds t_1+d, then a := t_2.

given.aim: Trace the SDD on the parse tree of a = b + c + d to generate the 3-address code.

terms.meaning: Left-recursive addition rule; b+c+d parses as (b+c)+d.
terms.example: b+c+d => E(b+c) + E(d) at top level.
terms.connects: Determines bottom-up reduction order: b+c is reduced first.

terms.term: Semantic action for E -> E_1 + E_2
terms.meaning: Allocate newtemp t, emit gen(t, ':=', E_1.place, '+', E_2.place), set E.place = t.
terms.example: For b+c: t_1 := b + c; E.place = t_1.
terms.connects: First reduction produces t_1 := b + c.

terms.term: Semantic action for S -> id = E
terms.meaning: Emit gen(id, ':=', E.place) — assigns the expression result to the LHS variable.
terms.example: For a = E with E.place = t_2: emits a := t_2.
terms.connects: Final instruction in the generated code.

given.plan: Parse b+c+d left-to-right using the left-recursive grammar. Reduce b to E (place=b), reduce c to E (place=c), then reduce E+E (b+c) to E using newtemp t_1 and gen. Reduce d to E (place=d), then reduce E+E (t_1+d) to E using newtemp t_2 and gen. Finally reduce a=E to S using gen.

steps.title: Build parse tree (left-associative)
steps.apply:
a = b + c + d is parsed as: S -> id(a) = E3
E3 -> E2 + id(d)
E2 -> id(b) + id(c)
So the left subtree E2 = b+c is reduced first in bottom-up parsing.
steps.note: Left recursion means leftmost addition is reduced first in bottom-up parsing.

steps.title: Reduce b and c to E
steps.apply:
E.place = b (for identifier b)
E.place = c (for identifier c)
No code generated yet.
steps.note: The E -> id rule just sets place; it does not call gen.

steps.title: Reduce E(b) + E(c) -> E using first newtemp
steps.formula_raw: t = newtemp(); gen(t, ':=', E_1.place, '+', E_2.place); E.place = t
steps.apply:
t_1 = newtemp() (first call)
gen(t_1, ':=', b, '+', c)  =>  t_1 := b + c
E2.place = t_1
steps.note: First 3-address instruction generated: t_1 := b + c.

steps.title: Reduce d to E, then E(t_1) + E(d) -> E
steps.apply:
E.place = d (for identifier d)
t_2 = newtemp() (second call)
gen(t_2, ':=', t_1, '+', d)  =>  t_2 := t_1 + d
E3.place = t_2

steps.title: Reduce S -> id(a) = E
steps.note: Final assignment instruction. No new temporary is needed here.

solution.result:
The 3-address code is:
t_1 := b + c
t_2 := t_1 + d
a := t_2
This matches option A.

questions.formula_note: Bottom-up (left-to-right) evaluation of left-recursive grammar for E produces left-associative grouping; each E -> E_1+E_2 reduction emits one 3-address instruction and allocates one temporary.

questions.to_find: The output printed by a bottom-up parser for input 'aab' using the given SDTS

understand.plain: The SDTS has three productions each with a print action. In a bottom-up parser, the semantic action fires when a reduction occurs. We trace through the shift-reduce parse of 'aab', noting which production is reduced at each step and what it prints.

keywords.explain: Reads input left-to-right, shifts tokens onto a stack, and reduces a matching right-hand side to a non-terminal when a complete production is found.
keywords.example: For a -> b + c, the parser shifts b, +, c then reduces to a.

keywords.explain: Code attached to a production that executes when the parser reduces by that production (in bottom-up parsing).
keywords.example: S -> a { print 2 } prints '2' when 'a' is reduced to S.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="400" height="220"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width="400" height="220" rx="8" class="bg"/><text x="200" y="26" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">Shift-Reduce trace for aab</text><rect x="10" y="38" width="160" height="24" rx="4" class="bg"/><rect x="175" y="38" width="120" height="24" rx="4" class="bg"/><rect x="300" y="38" width="90" height="24" rx="4" class="bg"/><text x="90" y="55" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">Stack</text><text x="235" y="55" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">Input</text><text x="345" y="55" text-anchor="middle" class="tag ink">Action</text><text x="90" y="82" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">$</text><text x="235" y="82" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">a a b $</text><text x="345" y="82" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">shift a</text><text x="90" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">$ a</text><text x="235" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">a b $</text><text x="345" y="106" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">shift a</text><text x="90" y="130" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">$ a a</text><text x="235" y="130" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">b $</text><text x="345" y="130" class="ok tag"><tspan x="345" text-anchor="middle">S->a (print 2)</tspan></text><text x="90" y="154" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">$ a S</text><text x="235" y="154" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">b $</text><text x="345" y="154" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">shift b</text><text x="90" y="178" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">$ a S b</text><text x="235" y="178" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">$</text><text x="345" y="178" class="ok tag"><tspan x="345" text-anchor="middle">A->Sb (print 3)</tspan></text><text x="90" y="202" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">$ a A</text><text x="235" y="202" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">$</text><text x="345" y="202" class="ok tag"><tspan x="345" text-anchor="middle">S->aA (print 1)</tspan></text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Shift-reduce parse table trace for input 'aab': shift a, shift a, reduce second a to S (print 2), shift b, reduce Sb to A (print 3), reduce aA to S (print 1). Output: 2 3 1.

given.aim: Trace the bottom-up (shift-reduce) parse of 'aab' and record the print output in order of reductions.

terms.meaning: S -> aA {print 1}, S -> a {print 2}, A -> Sb {print 3}
terms.example: When S -> a fires (reducing standalone 'a'), we print 2.
terms.connects: Each reduction fires its print action; order of reductions determines output order.

terms.meaning: Three tokens: a, a, b in sequence.
terms.example: Parser shifts a, a, then must decide when/what to reduce.
terms.connects: The second 'a' will be reduced to S before 'b' is consumed.

terms.meaning: Reductions happen innermost/rightmost-on-stack first; the deepest nested sub-expression is reduced first.
terms.example: In aab, the second 'a' at the bottom of the right subtree is reduced first.
terms.connects: Determines that print 2 fires first, then print 3, then print 1.

given.plan: Simulate the shift-reduce parse step by step: shift tokens, identify when a handle appears on top of the stack, perform the reduction and fire the print action. Record prints in order.

steps.title: Shift first 'a'
steps.apply:
Stack: $ a | Input remaining: a b $
No reduction possible yet (need to look ahead).
steps.note: The parser cannot yet tell if this 'a' should reduce to S or be part of S -> aA.

steps.title: Shift second 'a'
steps.apply:
Stack: $ a a | Input remaining: b $
Lookahead is 'b'. Top of stack is 'a'. The production S -> a applies to the top 'a'.
steps.note: The parser looks ahead: with 'b' coming, 'a' at top can reduce to S (needed for A -> Sb).

steps.title: Reduce: S -> a (print 2)
steps.apply:
Reduce top 'a' to S.
Stack: $ a S | Input: b $
Print: 2

steps.title: Shift 'b'
steps.apply:
Stack: $ a S b | Input: $
Top of stack matches right-hand side of A -> Sb.
steps.note: The handle Sb is now on top of the stack.

steps.title: Reduce: A -> Sb (print 3)
steps.apply:
Reduce Sb to A.
Stack: $ a A | Input: $
Print: 3

steps.title: Reduce: S -> aA (print 1)
steps.apply:
Reduce aA to S.
Stack: $ S | Input: $
Print: 1
Parsing complete (stack has start symbol S, input exhausted).
steps.note: Third output is '1'. The parse is successful.

solution.result: The output printed is: 2 3 1. Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: In bottom-up (shift-reduce) parsing, semantic actions fire at reduction time; the order of reductions determines the output sequence. Innermost/bottom-of-derivation reductions happen first.
